parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces substantial new runtime paths—retry logic (203 lines), BigModel error dispatch (168 lines), and a refactored client—while simultaneously deleting the `scripts/lib/process.mjs` module entirely. UAT coverage stops at v0.4.5; the v0.4.6 hardening changes (retry/backoff, error dispatch) have no corresponding UAT evidence. The deleted module creates a latent import-resolution crash risk that the modified import checker alone cannot guarantee is safe at runtime.

findings.title: Deleted process.mjs module risks runtime ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs was completely removed (67 lines deleted, 0 added). This module existed to export functionality consumed elsewhere. While scripts/check-imports.mjs was concurrently updated (5 lines changed), static import checking only validates module existence at lint time—it does not guarantee that every dynamic import() or conditional code path has been migrated. If glm-companion.mjs or any other runtime entry point still references './lib/process.mjs' under any execution branch, the result is an uncaught MODULE_NOT_FOUND at runtime. This is exactly the kind of failure that passes CI (which runs after install) but explodes in production on a specific code path.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase (including node_modules entry points and hook scripts) for the string 'process.mjs' or 'lib/process'. Verify that every former consumer of this module has been migrated to alternative implementations. Add an explicit runtime guard or smoke test that imports every module entry point to catch dangling references before they reach users.

findings.title: No UAT coverage for v0.4.6 retry/backoff and error-dispatch features
findings.body: The test-automation/uat-reports/ directory contains subdirectories for v0.4.4 and v0.4.5 only. There is no v0.4.6 UAT report or run script. The v0.4.6 release (commit e0a9ba8) introduces BigModel error dispatch (scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs, 168 new lines) and auto retry/backoff (scripts/lib/retry.mjs, 203 new lines). These are the highest-risk additions in the entire cumulative diff—retry logic is notoriously prone to edge-case failures (backoff overflow, retry-after parsing, non-idempotent retried operations, state corruption under concurrent retries). Shipping these without UAT evidence means the first real validation happens in production.
findings.recommendation: Create test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.6/ with scenarios that exercise: (1) transient API errors triggering retry with backoff, (2) permanent API errors correctly classified by bigmodel-errors and not retried, (3) max-retries-exceeded graceful degradation, (4) concurrent job retry state isolation. Run the UAT suite and attach evidence before shipping.

findings.title: Retry module integration with glm-client lacks observable failure mode
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs (203 new lines) and scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed) together form a new retry layer for external API calls. The retry.test.mjs (352 lines) suggests unit coverage exists, but the test file list shows no integration test (e.g., retry-client.test.mjs or similar) that validates the retry wrapper correctly handles the full request lifecycle—response body parsing failures after a successful retry, header propagation across retries, and error state when the client's own error classification disagrees with the retry policy. This is the class of bug where each component passes unit tests but the composition fails under real error conditions.
findings.recommendation: Add an integration test that mocks the HTTP layer to return a sequence of transient errors followed by success, and verify: (1) the final response is correctly returned to the caller, (2) retry count and backoff delays match expectations, (3) caller context (headers, body) is preserved across retries, (4) a permanent error mid-sequence is not retried and returns immediately with the correct error shape.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs and job-control.mjs changes lack concurrency safety evidence
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) and scripts/lib/job-control.mjs (20 lines changed) manage job lifecycle state. The new --wait/--background flags (from v0.4.5, commit d573807) change the execution model, which means the job state machine now has at least three modes (foreground, background, wait) with different invariants. The test files listed (job-render.test.mjs) appear to focus on rendering, not on state machine correctness under concurrent operations. If two operations race—e.g., a background job completing while a --wait polls—the tracked-jobs state file could be corrupted or return stale results.
findings.recommendation: Add a concurrency-focused test: start a background job, immediately issue a --wait, and verify the wait correctly resolves when the job completes. Also test the case where the job completes before the wait is issued (already-done state). Verify the state file is never written in a partially-valid state (atomic write or rename pattern).

next_steps: Run: grep -r 'process\.mjs\|lib/process' scripts/ commands/ hooks/ --include='*.mjs' --include='*.js' --include='*.json' to verify no dangling references to the deleted process.mjs module.
Execute the v0.4.6+ UAT scenarios: craft a test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.6/ directory with retry/backoff and error-classification scenarios and run them end-to-end.
Add an integration test exercising the retry.mjs + glm-client.mjs + bigmodel-errors.mjs pipeline under simulated transient and permanent API failures.
Verify atomic state-file writes in tracked-jobs.mjs to prevent corruption under concurrent background/wait operations.
